content.text:
Found 3 places where `Span` is modified directly instead of replacing it with a new `Span` from `Span.init*`:

1. [`State.add`, `aa` applies circumflex](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L260-L264)

   ```zig
   self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1].diacritic = .circumflex;
   ```

   This directly mutates `diacritic`.

2. [`State.add`, `ăa` overrides breve to circumflex](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L265-L269)

   ```zig
   self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1].diacritic = .circumflex;
   ```

   This directly mutates `diacritic`.

3. [`State.add`, `âa` cancels circumflex](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L270-L278)

   ```zig
   self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1].diacritic = .empty;
   ```

   This directly mutates `diacritic`.

The assignment in [`append_literal`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L336-L340) is correct because it replaces the whole `Span` using `Span.init(c)`:

```zig
self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length] = Span.init(c);
```

So the problematic pattern is only direct field assignment like:

```zig
span.diacritic = ...
```

Those should become whole-`Span` replacements, preserving the existing `base` and `tone` as needed.
I updated all places. Review again.

content.text:
Reviewed `src/liblex.zig` again.

No remaining direct `Span` field mutations found.

The previous wrong places now replace the full span correctly:

- [`aa` applies circumflex](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L260-L264)
- [`ăa` overrides breve to circumflex](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L266-L270)
- [`âa` cancels circumflex](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L272-L281)

These now use:

```zig
self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1] =
    Span.init_diacritic_tone(span_previous.base, ..., span_previous.tone);
```

The remaining `.base` / `.diacritic` usages are reads for branching or test assertions, not direct modifications. The assignment in [`append_literal`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L339-L343) is also correct because it assigns a whole new `Span` from `Span.init(c)`.
